features.explanation: This feature fires on individual Devanagari combining characters and punctuation marks (e.g., vowel signs like ा/ि/ी, halant ्, nukta ़, and commas/periods) as standalone tokens.
features.token_pattern: single-character tokens such as Devanagari vowel signs, virama (्), nukta (़), or punctuation like commas and periods
features.context_pattern: appears across arbitrary sentences with no shared semantic theme, triggered purely by specific character-level tokens
features.corpus_consistency: yes, it consistently fires on the same types of character-level tokens across flores and xsafety

features.explanation: This feature fires on very short high-frequency tokens, especially single-character tokens like commas and the Devanagari vowel sign "ा".
features.token_pattern: Peak activations are single-character tokens such as ',' or the Devanagari vowel sign 'ा' (and similar short orthographic pieces).
features.context_pattern: Appears in arbitrary sentence positions across many topics, with no consistent semantic theme beyond containing these short tokens.
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it consistently fires on the same type of short orthographic tokens across both flores and xsafety corpora.

features.explanation: This feature fires on common Hindi function words and grammatical markers such as conjunctions, relative pronouns, postpositions, and short particles (e.g., और, या, जो, से, एक).
features.token_pattern: short high-frequency grammatical tokens or morphemes, often conjunctions, particles, postpositions, or inflectional endings
features.context_pattern: appears at clause boundaries, in lists, relative clauses, and prepositional/postpositional phrases linking sentence elements
features.corpus_consistency: appears consistently on the same class of grammatical function words across the provided flores examples

features.explanation: This feature fires on standalone Devanagari characters—especially vowel matras (ा, ी), the virama (्), and similar single-character tokens—produced by the tokenizer.
features.token_pattern: single-character Devanagari combining marks or very short orthographic tokens (e.g., ा, ी, ्, comma)
features.context_pattern: appears in arbitrary Hindi sentences with no shared semantic theme, only the presence of these standalone character tokens
features.corpus_consistency: yes, it consistently fires on the same type of single-character tokens across flores and xsafety

features.explanation: This feature activates on Hindi words related to deception, fraud, cheating, and theft (e.g., "धोखा", "धोखाधड़ी", "चोरी", "हेराफेरी").
features.token_pattern: Devanagari tokens that are parts of words like "धोखा", "धोखाधड़ी", "चोरी", or other fraud-related terms, often the "खा/ख/ा" segments within these words
features.context_pattern: Contexts describing being cheated, committing fraud, financial scams, theft, or accusations of dishonesty
features.safety_relevance: Related to financial crime, fraud, and deceptive behavior, which are safety-relevant harm categories
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it consistently fires on deception/fraud concepts across the provided xsafety examples

features.explanation: This feature fires on Devanagari continuation subword tokens such as matras (ा, ि, ी), virama (्), and other non-space-prefixed fragments or punctuation like commas.
features.token_pattern: individual Devanagari combining marks, vowel signs, virama, or other subword tokens that do not begin a new space-delimited word, often including commas
features.context_pattern: appears across diverse sentences with no shared semantic theme, triggered purely by specific orthographic subword fragments
features.corpus_consistency: yes, the same type of orthographic subword tokens activate it in both flores and xsafety

features.explanation: This feature fires on Devanagari combining marks and mid-word subword pieces (e.g., vowel matras, virama, nukta, or continuation tokens) rather than standalone whole words.
features.token_pattern: Single-character vowel signs (ा, ी, ि), virama (्), nukta (़), punctuation, or subword fragments that attach to a preceding character (no leading space).
features.context_pattern: Occurs inside ordinary Hindi words across diverse sentences, typically at non-initial positions within a word.
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it consistently fires on the same type of orthographic continuation tokens in both flores and xsafety.

features.explanation: Activates on Devanagari tokens that are part of English loanwords or foreign proper nouns transliterated into Hindi, especially technical terms.
features.token_pattern: Subword pieces like ट, टा, टिंग, इन, टर, ी that commonly appear inside transliterated English words (e.g., डेटिंग, डेटा, ऑनलाइन, कंप्यूटर, टैक्सी).
features.context_pattern: Sentences containing English-derived technical terms, job titles, place names, or modern concepts written in Devanagari script.
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it consistently fires on transliterated English words across both flores and xsafety corpora.

features.explanation: This feature fires on common high-frequency Devanagari subword tokens, especially vowel matras and punctuation like commas, rather than a semantic concept.
features.token_pattern: Single-character Devanagari vowel signs (ा, ि, ी, ्), common suffix fragments, and punctuation such as commas
features.context_pattern: Appears across arbitrary Hindi sentences regardless of topic, activating on frequent character-level tokens within words or at punctuation marks
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it fires on the same kinds of common character-level tokens in both flores and xsafety corpora.

features.explanation: This feature fires on specific Devanagari combining characters (e.g., vowel matras, virama, nukta) and also strongly on punctuation like commas, indicating a low-level orthographic token pattern.
features.token_pattern: individual Devanagari combining marks (ा, ि, ी, ्, ़) and punctuation tokens such as commas
features.context_pattern: appears across arbitrary sentences regardless of topic, with no consistent semantic theme beyond containing these specific characters
features.corpus_consistency: yes, it fires on the same orthographic token types in both flores and xsafety corpora

features.explanation: This feature fires on very short standalone tokens in Hindi text, especially single Devanagari characters (matras/virama), punctuation marks, and digits.
features.token_pattern: peak tokens are single Unicode characters or very short tokens (e.g., ा, ी, ्, ,, 1)
features.context_pattern: appears across arbitrary sentences where isolated characters, punctuation, or digits occur within otherwise normal Hindi text
features.corpus_consistency: yes, it activates on the same type of short standalone tokens in both flores and xsafety corpora

features.explanation: This feature fires on Devanagari combining vowel signs and diacritics (matras, virama, nukta) attached to consonants in Hindi text.
features.token_pattern: single Devanagari combining marks such as ा, ि, ी, ्, ़ or tokens immediately adjacent to them
features.context_pattern: appears בתוך ordinary Hindi words across varied topics, independent of semantic content

features.explanation: Activates on individual Devanagari combining characters such as vowel matras and the virama, often as standalone subword tokens.
features.token_pattern: single Devanagari diacritics or combining marks (e.g., ा, ि, ी, ्) and similar short script-level tokens
features.context_pattern: appears within ordinary Hindi words regardless of topic, with no consistent semantic theme
features.corpus_consistency: yes, it consistently fires on the same type of Devanagari character tokens across flores and xsafety

features.explanation: Fires on words expressing desire, preference, approval or disapproval such as 'इच्छा', 'पसंद/नापसंद', 'अस्वीकृति', 'अनुचित', and 'अनचाहे'.
features.token_pattern: peak tokens are characters within evaluative words about wanting, liking, rejecting, or appropriateness
features.context_pattern: sentences discuss personal desires, likes/dislikes, rejection, criticism, or judgments about what is appropriate or unwanted
features.safety_relevance: appears in contexts about inappropriate behavior or harmful preferences but mainly marks general evaluative/desire language
features.corpus_consistency: yes, the same desire/approval concept appears in both flores and xsafety examples

features.explanation: This feature fires on the Devanagari vowel sign 'े' when it forms the masculine/plural habitual verb ending '-ते', especially in forms like 'करते'.
features.token_pattern: The token 'े' occurring as part of the verb ending 'ते', most often in 'करते'.
features.context_pattern: Appears in habitual or participial verb phrases such as 'करते हैं', 'करते हुए', and 'करते समय'.
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it consistently activates on the same '-ते' verb ending across both flores and xsafety corpora.

features.explanation: This feature fires on standalone Devanagari combining characters and diacritics (e.g., ा, ी, ्) and similar single-character subword tokens within Hindi text.
features.token_pattern: single-character Devanagari vowel signs, virama (्), or similar non-initial subword tokens (occasionally punctuation like comma)
features.context_pattern: appears inside Hindi words as part of grapheme clusters or syllables across general prose in both corpora
features.corpus_consistency: yes, it consistently activates on Devanagari combining marks across both flores and xsafety

features.explanation: This feature activates on words related to knowledge, information, and finding out facts (e.g., जानकारी, पता, ज्ञान, वैज्ञानिक).
features.token_pattern: Tokens that are part of Hindi words like 'जानकारी', 'पता', 'ज्ञान', or 'वैज्ञानिक', often the 'ना/न/ा' within these knowledge-related words.
features.context_pattern: Sentences discussing knowing something, obtaining information, scientific knowledge, discovering facts, or referencing personal information.
features.safety_relevance: Often appears in contexts about personal information and privacy (e.g., misuse of personal data), which is relevant to safety and privacy concerns.
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it consistently fires on knowledge/information-related concepts across both flores and xsafety corpora.

features.explanation: This feature primarily fires on specific Devanagari characters within words related to fraud, deception, and corruption (e.g., धोखा, झूठ, भ्रष्टाचार).
features.token_pattern: individual Devanagari characters (e.g., ख, ठ, ैं, ी) occurring inside words about fraud or lying
features.context_pattern: contexts discussing cheating, fraud, lying, corruption, or deceptive behavior
features.safety_relevance: associated with discussions of fraud, deception, and corrupt behavior in harmful or unethical scenarios
features.corpus_consistency: mostly consistent across xsafety and flores, centering on deception/corruption themes

features.explanation: This feature strongly activates on the Devanagari vowel sign "ा" (aa matra) and similar very short orthographic tokens like commas or halant marks, independent of semantic context.
features.token_pattern: single-character tokens, especially the Devanagari vowel sign "ा" (and occasionally punctuation like commas or halant "्")
features.context_pattern: appears across arbitrary Hindi sentences wherever the character-level token occurs, regardless of topic or meaning
features.corpus_consistency: yes, it fires on the same character-level pattern in both flores and xsafety corpora

features.explanation: This feature fires on Hindi pronouns, especially personal and demonstrative forms like 'मैं', 'आप', 'वह', and 'यह'.
features.token_pattern: Standalone Devanagari pronouns or their core tokens (e.g., मैं/ैं, आप, वह, यह, उन्हें/ें)
features.context_pattern: Sentences involving personal reference, narration, or direct address where speakers refer to themselves or others
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it consistently activates on pronouns across both flores and xsafety corpora.

features.explanation: This feature activates on word-final characters in Hindi, especially the last consonant or nasal marker (e.g., द, न, म, ं) at the end of a word.
features.token_pattern: tokens that are the final character of a word, often consonants or nasalization markers like ं, न, द, म
features.context_pattern: appears at word boundaries immediately before a space or punctuation, regardless of semantic content
features.corpus_consistency: yes, it consistently fires on word-final characters across both flores and xsafety corpora

features.explanation: This feature fires on first-person singular self-references in Hindi, especially the pronoun "मैं" and associated verb forms like "हूँ" in spoken or quoted statements.

features.context_pattern: Quoted or narrative sentences where a speaker refers to themselves using first-person singular constructions (e.g., "मैं ... हूँ", "मुझे", "मैं ...")

features.explanation: This feature activates on Hindi verb inflectional endings, especially common sentence-final suffixes like "ा", "े", "ूं", and the copula "है".
features.token_pattern: Peak tokens are verb suffix morphemes such as ा, े, ूं, and forms of है that mark tense, aspect, number, or person.
features.context_pattern: They occur within finite verb phrases, often at the end of clauses or sentences, including first-person statements and narrative past-tense descriptions.
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it consistently fires on verb inflectional endings across both flores and xsafety corpora.

features.explanation: This feature activates on tokens that occur at the beginning of a new word (i.e., immediately following a space or sentence boundary) in Hindi text.
features.token_pattern: tokens that start a new word, typically preceded by a space or punctuation boundary
features.context_pattern: appears across arbitrary sentences wherever a new word begins, regardless of semantic content
features.corpus_consistency: yes, it consistently fires on word-initial tokens in both flores and xsafety corpora

features.explanation: This feature activates on the Hindi demonstrative pronoun "यह" ("this").
features.token_pattern: The peak token is the standalone word "यह".
features.context_pattern: Appears in sentences where "यह" functions as a demonstrative pronoun, often in constructions like "यह भी", "यह बताना", or introducing a clause or proposition.

features.explanation: This feature activates on common Hindi inflectional suffixes and final matras that mark gender, number, or tense on nouns, adjectives, and verbs.

features.explanation: This feature fires on standalone Devanagari combining characters (matras/virama) and similar non-initial tokens such as commas that attach to preceding text.

features.explanation: This feature fires on common Devanagari subword chunks that form parts of content words, especially nominal or verbal stems split by BPE (e.g., -न, -क, -जन, -तक, -ि).
features.token_pattern: frequent mid-word Devanagari consonant-vowel subword pieces within nouns and verbs
features.context_pattern: appears inside common content words (nouns, gerunds, technical terms) across varied topics

features.explanation: This feature activates on Sanskrit-derived adjectival forms like 'व्यक्तिगत', 'सामाजिक', 'भौतिक', and similar words denoting abstract qualities or categories (personal, social, physical, moral, etc.).
features.token_pattern: Subword tokens forming the adjectival suffixes '-िक' or '-गत' in words like 'व्यक्तिगत', 'सामाजिक', 'भौतिक', 'नैतिक', 'पारिवारिक'.
features.context_pattern: Contexts discussing abstract attributes, categories, or qualities such as personal information, social activities, moral values, physical objects, or professional/familial relations.

features.explanation: This feature activates on Hindi interrogative words like "क्या" and "क्यों" that introduce questions.

features.context_pattern: sentences phrased as direct questions, typically ending with a question mark and asking for information
features.corpus_consistency: yes, it fires on interrogative forms in both flores and xsafety examples, though more frequently in xsafety

features.explanation: This feature activates on coordinating conjunctions and clause-linking punctuation (especially 'और' and commas) within emotionally negative or distress-related narratives.
features.token_pattern: peak tokens are usually the conjunction 'और', commas ',', or common clause-connecting morphemes like 'े'
features.context_pattern: sentences describing sadness, helplessness, disappointment, or personal distress where multiple negative feelings or events are linked together

features.explanation: This feature fires on Devanagari vowel matra characters (e.g., ा, ी, े, ि) that typically appear as inflectional suffixes in Hindi words.

features.corpus_consistency: yes, it consistently activates on the same vowel diacritic characters across both flores and xsafety corpora

features.explanation: This feature fires on the Hindi word "एक" used as the numeral or indefinite article meaning "one" or "a/an" before a noun.

features.explanation: This feature fires on individual Devanagari character tokens (often single consonants or diacritics) produced by subword splitting, independent of semantic meaning.

features.explanation: Activates on Hindi interrogative constructions, especially question endings like "है?", "हैं?", "क्यों", and "कैसे".
features.token_pattern: Peak tokens are common interrogative or question-final forms such as "है", "हैं", "यों" (in "क्यों"), "ें" (in "करें"), and "े" (in "कैसे"), typically appearing right before a question mark.
features.context_pattern: Sentences are direct questions containing interrogative words like "क्या", "क्यों", "कैसे" and ending with a question mark.
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it consistently fires on question constructions in both flores and xsafety corpora.

features.explanation: This feature fires on Hindi present-tense verb and copula constructions, especially agreement endings and auxiliaries like 'है/हैं/हूँ' at clause boundaries.
features.token_pattern: Verb agreement suffixes and copula forms (ी, ा, े, ूं, है, हैं, हूँ) and nearby clause punctuation
features.context_pattern: Declarative sentences describing habitual or ongoing actions, often with coordinated clauses (e.g., '... आता है, और ... होता हूँ')
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it consistently activates on the same present-tense/copula constructions in both flores and xsafety corpora.

features.explanation: This feature fires on common Devanagari inflectional endings and function-word tokens that frequently appear in first-person singular statements (e.g., "मैं", "मेरे", "चाहता हूँ").
features.token_pattern: High-frequency Devanagari matras and letters (ैं, े, ा, ि, ो, न, त) forming parts of words like "मैं", "मेरे", "अपने", "चाहिए", "रहता"
features.context_pattern: Sentences with first-person self-references and desire/intention constructions (e.g., "मैं ... चाहता हूँ", "मैं ... रहता हूँ")
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it consistently activates on the same first-person grammatical patterns across xsafety examples.

features.explanation: This feature fires on forms of the Hindi copular/auxiliary verb "होना" (e.g., है, हूँ, हो, हैं, था), especially their initial character tokens.
features.token_pattern: the Devanagari characters ह or थ as part of forms like है, हूँ, हो, हैं, था
features.context_pattern: declarative sentences expressing states, properties, or habitual actions using the auxiliary verb "to be"
features.corpus_consistency: yes, it consistently activates on copula/auxiliary verb forms across both flores and xsafety corpora

features.explanation: This feature activates on the Hindi complementizer "कि" used to introduce a subordinate clause, especially after verbs of thinking, feeling, or saying (e.g., "लगता है कि", "है कि").
features.token_pattern: The token "क" as part of the bigram "कि", typically in constructions like "है कि", "था कि", or "लगता है कि".
features.context_pattern: Appears immediately after cognitive or perceptual predicates introducing a reported thought, belief, feeling, or statement in a subordinate clause.
features.corpus_consistency: Yes, it consistently fires on the same "कि" complementizer construction across both flores and xsafety corpora.

features.explanation: This feature primarily activates on comma punctuation and similar clause-boundary tokens in Hindi text.

features.explanation: This feature activates on word-final Devanagari characters, especially the last akshar of adjectives, adverbs, and participles (e.g., शामिल, ज्यादा, छोटा, निरंतर, बहुत).

features.context_pattern: appears at the end of descriptive or modifying words within otherwise normal narrative or instructional sentences

features.explanation: This feature activates on individual Devanagari character tokens, especially vowel diacritics (matras), the halant sign, and punctuation like commas, independent of semantic content.

features.explanation: This feature fires on standalone Devanagari combining marks and similar high-frequency single-character tokens such as "ा", "्", "ी", and sometimes punctuation like commas.
